caveats: a: No representative occupation mix or wage-weighted task study is available for 814110; BLS OEWS explicitly excludes household workers.
a: The mix between nannies, cleaners, cooks, aides, drivers, and grounds workers is unknown and materially affects exposure.
a: Administrative tasks may be performed unpaid by household members rather than included in employee wages.
rho: No source measures AI implementation in directly employed private-household work.
rho: Households may use AI for unpaid coordination without reducing paid physical or care hours.
rho: Safety, trust, and privacy constraints differ sharply across childcare, personal care, cooking, cleaning, driving, and grounds work.
e: A household may be wealthy and employ several workers, but it is still a consuming household rather than a service firm with normalized EBITDA.
e: Workers may be recruited through an agency while remaining household employees, but the household still does not sell services externally.
e: Service agencies and self-employed providers can be investable businesses, but they are classified outside 814110.
s5: Household wealth transfers and estate events are outside the qualifying-control-transfer construct.
s5: Sales of domestic staffing or household-service agencies occur in other industry classifications.
q: A household can retain personal savings from reduced paid hours, but that is consumer expenditure savings rather than operator commercial retention.
q: Payroll-service or staffing-agency economics belong to firms classified outside 814110.
d5: BLS employment is a labor-input proxy, not constant-price, constant-quality demand.
d5: Official payroll data may miss informal household employment and shifts between direct employment, agencies, contractors, and unpaid household work.
d5: Demand paths differ across childcare, elder support, cleaning, cooking, driving, and grounds work.
o: Human presence and the roll-up operator construct are different: a nanny or housekeeper may remain necessary without creating an investable firm in this code.
o: Agency-provided services can require accountable operators, but those agencies are classified outside 814110.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Households are employers and pay employees wages hourly, daily, weekly, or by the job; they may owe payroll taxes and keep employment records. The classified household is not an operating firm selling a recurring outsourced service to external customers, so the screen's customer-revenue and transferable-firm constructs do not apply coherently.
lens.deviation_from_default: none; the default lens produces no eligible operating-firm population because NAICS 814110 classifies the household employer itself, while independently controlled agencies and service businesses are classified elsewhere
lens.excluded_activities: Agencies or independent businesses that control and supply childcare, cleaning, lawn, personal-care, nursing, or other household services to customers; self-employed workers offering services to the public; non-medical personal-care providers and child day-care establishments classified elsewhere; households without employees; and any activity outside the target external-customer LMM firm lens.
lens.included_activities: The official NAICS 814110 population: U.S. private households directly employing workers on or about the premises for household operation, including nannies, babysitters, cooks, maids, housekeepers, butlers, caretakers, drivers, non-medical aides, private nurses, gardeners, and other yard or maintenance workers.

caveats: No representative occupation mix or wage-weighted task study is available for 814110; BLS OEWS explicitly excludes household workers.
The mix between nannies, cleaners, cooks, aides, drivers, and grounds workers is unknown and materially affects exposure.
Administrative tasks may be performed unpaid by household members rather than included in employee wages.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted occupation and task-time study of workers directly employed by private households, including current use of consumer software and household robotics, would materially change the estimate.
a.rationale: Scheduling, shopping and meal planning, household records, payroll reminders, communications, routine monitoring, and activity planning can be assisted by AI. Most paid work named in the classification involves physical cleaning, cooking, childcare, personal attendance, driving, grounds work, supervision, safety, trust, and presence in the home.

d5.bridge: Source population/quantity: BLS projects private-household employment from 650,400 in 2024 to 653,000 in 2034, a 0.4% ten-year increase, for the official Census service population. Transformation: treated the near-flat employment path as a directional anchor for a five-year quantity ratio centered at current demand and widened it for productivity, hours, informality, service-channel substitution, demographics, and household income. Error direction: employment may understate service quantity if productivity rises and may misstate direct-household demand when work shifts to agencies, contractors, robotics, or unpaid household members.
caveats: BLS employment is a labor-input proxy, not constant-price, constant-quality demand.
Official payroll data may miss informal household employment and shifts between direct employment, agencies, contractors, and unpaid household work.
Demand paths differ across childcare, elder support, cleaning, cooking, driving, and grounds work.
d5.change_evidence: A constant-price five-year series of direct-household paid hours and active household employers by duty, separated from agencies, independent contractors, and informal unpaid work, would materially change the estimate.
d5.rationale: Demand for childcare, cleaning, cooking, personal attendance, and household maintenance should persist, while demographics, household income, immigration and labor supply, remote work, service-agency substitution, informal work, and household technology can move paid direct employment in either direction. BLS projects essentially flat private-household employment over 2024-2034.

e.bridge: Source population/quantity: Census defines every 814110 establishment as a private household employing workers for household operation, and IRS guidance distinguishes those employees from self-employed businesses and workers controlled by an agency. Transformation: mapped the categorical absence of an external-customer operating firm inside the official industry boundary to a zero eligible-firm share under the frozen lens. Error direction: misclassified service agencies could make zero too low in administrative data, but correctly classified agencies belong to other NAICS codes and do not change the 814110 construct.
caveats: A household may be wealthy and employ several workers, but it is still a consuming household rather than a service firm with normalized EBITDA.
Workers may be recruited through an agency while remaining household employees, but the household still does not sell services externally.
Service agencies and self-employed providers can be investable businesses, but they are classified outside 814110.
e.change_evidence: A classification ruling showing that independently controlled operating firms with external customers are included in 814110, rather than in the relevant cleaning, childcare, personal-care, landscaping, staffing, or other service code, would change this structural value.
e.rationale: The official units in 814110 are private households employing workers, not firms supplying services to external customers. IRS guidance likewise distinguishes household employees from self-employed workers and agency-controlled workers, leaving no establishment population that satisfies the default external-service LMM firm lens within this code.

l.detail: SUSB scope excludes this activity, no IPS labor-compensation/output series at any level, and no other receipts source downloaded; labor_share left null (recorded gap).
l.rationale: dataset labor_share has no defensible value

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[81]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $170,393 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 81); DATA GAP: no SUSB size-class data for this code; n_band could not be computed — the research run must supply it with a shown chain, quality ESTIMATE
n.rationale: dataset n_band has no defensible value

caveats: Human presence and the roll-up operator construct are different: a nanny or housekeeper may remain necessary without creating an investable firm in this code.
Agency-provided services can require accountable operators, but those agencies are classified outside 814110.
o.change_evidence: A defensible mapping of 814110 service quantity to correctly classified external operating firms, with household cohorts comparing direct employment, self-service, agencies, contractors, and software or robotics, would be required to estimate o.
o.rationale: Physical household work often still requires a responsible person, but the official operator is a household employee rather than an accountable firm of the frozen lens's kind. The share requiring such a firm is therefore undefined for 814110.

caveats: A household can retain personal savings from reduced paid hours, but that is consumer expenditure savings rather than operator commercial retention.
Payroll-service or staffing-agency economics belong to firms classified outside 814110.
q.change_evidence: Correctly classified 814110 firm contracts showing external customers, implemented gross benefits, pricing, renewals, pass-through, and retained contribution margin would be required to estimate q.
q.rationale: The classified household pays wages and employment taxes rather than selling an outsourced service to customers, so there is no operator gross benefit, customer pass-through, or renewal repricing relationship from which to estimate commercial retention.

caveats: No source measures AI implementation in directly employed private-household work.
Households may use AI for unpaid coordination without reducing paid physical or care hours.
Safety, trust, and privacy constraints differ sharply across childcare, personal care, cooking, cleaning, driving, and grounds work.
rho.change_evidence: A five-year panel of household employers and workers measuring paid hours, task mix, consumer-AI and robotics adoption, errors, safety incidents, service quality, privacy concerns, and household spending would materially change the estimate.
rho.rationale: Consumer AI tools can be adopted quickly for scheduling, planning, translation, records, instructions, and reminders, but direct household employment is fragmented and lacks standardized systems. Privacy, trust, safety, variable homes, worker access to technology, and the physical nature of duties constrain realized labor substitution.

caveats: Household wealth transfers and estate events are outside the qualifying-control-transfer construct.
Sales of domestic staffing or household-service agencies occur in other industry classifications.
s5.change_evidence: Evidence of correctly classified 814110 operating firms with transferable equity, together with a five-year cohort of their completed control transactions, would be required to estimate s5.
s5.rationale: There is no eligible firm population within the frozen lens, so a probability of qualifying control transfer is undefined rather than economically zero. A household move, death, inheritance, or change of domestic employee is not a control sale of an operating service firm.

principal_driver: The principal driver is persistent household need for trusted, physical care and upkeep, with a small layer of AI-assistable planning and administration.
principal_weakness: The principal weakness is structural: NAICS 814110 contains household employers rather than external-customer operating firms that can be acquired and rolled up.

title: Private Households
